steps.reveal: omnium = of all things (gen. pl., partitive)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'omnes/omnia' — 'omnium' with -ium is genitive
hints.accusative: Accusative plural would be 'omnes/omnia' — 'omnium' is genitive
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'omnibus' — 'omnium' is genitive

hints.to stand together: While etymologically 'con + stare', in this usage 'constare' means 'to be agreed/established'
hints.to cost: 'Constare' can mean 'to cost', but with 'satis' it means 'it is sufficiently established'
hints.to consist: That would be closer to 'consistere' — here 'constare' means 'to be agreed'

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'constitit' — 'constat' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'constet' — 'constat' with -at is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'constabit' — 'constat' is present indicative

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand) — 'constare' is its compound con + stare
hints.consisto, consistere, constiti, constitu: These are the parts of 'consistere' (to stop/take a stand)
hints.constituo, constituere, constitui, const: These are the parts of 'constituere' (to establish/decide)

steps.reveal: Troia = Troy (abl., in ablative absolute with 'capta')

hints.nominative: While 'Troia' could be nominative, here it forms an ablative absolute with 'capta'
hints.vocative: Livy is narrating, not addressing Troy
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Troiam' — 'Troia' is ablative in the ablative absolute

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'Troiae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Troiae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'Troiae' with -ae is 1st declension

steps.reveal: capta = having been captured (abl. fem. sg.)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'capiens' — 'capta' with -ta is perfect passive
hints.future active participle: Future active participle would be 'captura' — 'capta' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'capienda' — 'capta' is perfect passive participle

hints.to hold: That would be 'tenere' — 'capta' comes from 'capere' (to take/capture)
hints.to head for: That would be 'petere' — 'capta' comes from 'capere' (to take/capture)
hints.to begin: That would be 'incipere' — 'capta' comes from 'capere' (to take/capture)

hints.teneo, tenere, tenui, tentum: These are the parts of 'tenere' (to hold)
hints.rapio, rapere, rapui, raptum: These are the parts of 'rapere' (to snatch/seize)
hints.accipio, accipere, accepi, acceptum: These are the parts of 'accipere' (to receive) — a compound of capere

translation: First of all, it is sufficiently agreed that, Troy having been captured
